evidence: ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks uses long[] state = {0, 0, 0} with implicit index semantics (0=sentences, 1=tokens, 2=chunks) — magic indices instead of named fields

ConlluConverter.processLines uses Optional<BufferedWriter> to represent nullable mutable writer state — Optional is not designed for this use case; a plain @Nullable BufferedWriter field with null checks is more idiomatic Java

rotateChunkIfNeeded returns Optional<long[]> as a control signal ('rotation happened, here is the new count') — an unusual encoding; a boolean return with a mutable count parameter, or a small result record, would be clearer

GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition does custom single-pass bracket scanning (tracking depth, looking for label char + colon) — partially duplicates the bracket-walking already in CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens without delegation
RelationPatternBuilder.posGroupForPrefix uses 10+ string.contains() checks in if/else chains — a pattern→PosGroup lookup Map would be more direct and extensible

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern (the full implementation) is cleanly decomposed into 4 phase helpers (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) with clear single responsibilities
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens handles nested brackets and quoted strings correctly with a depth-counting approach — complex but well-documented and appropriately tested
MultiSeedExplorer.computeCollocateIntersection is clearly decomposed into phase helpers with phase-comment headers, making the algorithm easy to follow

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore has an inline comment '// Safe: parseRelationConfig guarantees relationType is present' — correct but the safety depends on a cross-method invariant that the type system does not encode

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer algorithm decomposed cleanly into named phase helpers with single responsibilities
MultiSeedExplorer phase helpers (fetchCollocatesPerSeed, identifyCommonCollocates, buildDiscoveredNouns, buildCoreCollocates) are intention-revealing
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens handles edge cases (nested brackets, quoted strings) correctly with clear state tracking
GrammarConfigLoader private methods are appropriately sized and named with no monolithic parse method
low_level_elegance.issue_character: The main low-level elegance issues are concentrated in ConlluConverter, which uses Optional<T> to model mutable nullable state (a non-idiomatic Java pattern) and a raw indexed array for multi-field state — both of which obscure intent and create fragile implicit contracts.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The majority of the codebase is well-structured at the function level with clean decomposition and clear state management. The score is in the low 70s because ConlluConverter contains two distinct implementation-craft issues (Optional as nullable-state holder, indexed long[] for named state), GrammarConfigLoader has a bracket-walking reimplementation that partially duplicates CqlUtils, and RelationPatternBuilder has a string-matching if-chain where a lookup table would be cleaner. These are concrete readability and correctness-risk issues, not style preferences.

issues.summary: ConlluConverter uses long[] with implicit index semantics for multi-field mutable state instead of named fields
evidence: long[] state = {0, 0, 0}; // [sentences, tokens, chunks] — the comment is the only documentation for the magic indices
state[0]++, state[1]++, state[2] are accessed throughout processLines and rotateChunkIfNeeded
rotateChunkIfNeeded returns Optional.of(new long[]{chunks + 1}) and the caller assigns state[2] = rotated.get()[0] — the index coupling is fragile
issues.suggestion: Replace long[] state with a private mutable ConversionState class or record with fields sentenceCount, tokenCount, chunkCount. Pass it by reference through processLines, eliminating the index magic and the confusing rotateChunkIfNeeded Optional<long[]> return value.

issues.summary: ConlluConverter.processLines uses Optional<BufferedWriter> to represent nullable mutable writer state — non-idiomatic Java
evidence: Optional<BufferedWriter> writerOpt = Optional.empty(); declared as a local variable reassigned throughout processLines
writerOpt = Optional.of(openChunk(...)): reassigning Optional wrappers in a loop is not idiomatic — Optional is designed for method return values, not mutable container state
if (writerOpt.isEmpty()) / writerOpt.get().write() called repeatedly — null check pattern via isEmpty/get is less readable than a plain @Nullable BufferedWriter with explicit null checks
issues.suggestion: Replace Optional<BufferedWriter> writerOpt with @Nullable BufferedWriter writer = null. Use explicit null checks (if (writer == null)) and direct assignment (writer = openChunk(...)). This is more idiomatic Java and makes the mutability explicit.

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition reimplements bracket-walking logic already available in CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens
evidence: GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition manually tracks bracket depth and scans for label characters (e.g. '1:') in a custom loop
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens already walks brackets and returns an ordered token list — the same token list from which position can be derived by index
The CqlUtils.java Javadoc notes that GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition 'extends this logic'
issues.suggestion: Rewrite deriveTokenPosition to call CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens(pattern) and find the token index where the raw pattern text before the token starts with the label character + colon. This removes the duplicate bracket-walking and keeps bracket logic in one place.

root_causes: ConlluConverter was written with Optional<T> as nullable state before a local mutable variable pattern was established; indexed array state avoids introducing a new class
